critic_raw:
The class balancing strategy is unspecified — oversampling before the temporal split causes synthetic duplicates across train/validation/test partitions, constituting leakage. If balancing occurs after splitting, calibration implications are unaddressed.

Surgeon ID as a fixed effect feature allows test-time lookup of surgeon-specific outcome patterns from training — the model cannot generalize to new surgeons and the evaluation is optimistic for deployment scenarios involving new surgeons.

The pretrained clinical language model fine-tuned on MIMIC may overlap temporally or institutionally with the study data, introducing distributional contamination not assessed in the design.
all_issues_raised: The class balancing strategy is unspecified — if oversampling occurs before the temporal split, synthetic duplicates contaminate validation and test sets; if after, calibration implications for predicted probability interpretation are unaddressed.

Surgeon ID as a fixed effect feature enables test-time lookup of surgeon-specific outcome patterns learned from training data, meaning the model cannot generalize to new surgeons and the test evaluation is optimistic for deployment scenarios involving surgeon turnover.

The pretrained language model fine-tuned on MIMIC may have been exposed to notes from the same hospital system or time period as the study, introducing distributional contamination that is not assessed.

round_transcripts.critic:
The class balancing strategy is unspecified — oversampling before the temporal split causes synthetic duplicates across train/validation/test partitions, constituting leakage. If balancing occurs after splitting, calibration implications are unaddressed.

Surgeon ID as a fixed effect feature allows test-time lookup of surgeon-specific outcome patterns from training — the model cannot generalize to new surgeons and the evaluation is optimistic for deployment scenarios involving new surgeons.

The pretrained clinical language model fine-tuned on MIMIC may overlap temporally or institutionally with the study data, introducing distributional contamination not assessed in the design.
round_transcripts.defender:
Class balancing through cost-sensitive weighting (class weights inversely proportional to class frequency) requires no resampling and introduces no leakage regardless of when it is applied — it is the standard approach for imbalanced clinical outcomes and avoids the synthetic sample problem entirely. The design's reference to balancing most naturally implies this approach.

Surgeon ID as a fixed effect is a standard technique in surgical outcome research to control for surgeon-level confounding. In a within-hospital deployment scenario, all surgeons in test data have been observed during training — this is realistic for the intended deployment. The evaluation appropriately tests whether the model generalizes to future patients operated on by the same surgeons. Generalization to new surgeons is a separate scope question.

The MIMIC pretraining contamination concern is hypothetical — MIMIC draws from BIDMC in Boston, which is a specific institution. Unless the study is conducted at BIDMC (which would be a known design parameter), there is no institutional overlap. Temporal overlap in document type does not constitute contamination of the specific patient population.

round_transcripts.critic:
The defense's presumption that balancing implies cost-sensitive weighting is plausible but the design explicitly says 'balance these against uncomplicated cases,' which implies case selection (undersampling or oversampling), not weighting. This should be explicitly specified.

The MIMIC contamination concern is substantially resolved by the institutional specificity argument — BIDMC is a different institution from most study sites.

The surgeon fixed effect issue is partially resolved by the within-hospital deployment framing, but the design should explicitly note that deployment performance for new surgeons is untested.
round_transcripts.defender: The balancing language can be interpreted as either approach; specifying cost-sensitive weighting in the implementation would resolve the ambiguity. The surgeon generalization limitation is a documented scope boundary, not a methodological error.

all_issues_adjudicated: The class balancing strategy is unspecified — if oversampling occurs before the temporal split, synthetic duplicates contaminate validation and test sets; if after, calibration implications for predicted probability interpretation are unaddressed.
